period_searched: March 5–19, 2026 (48-hour priority; expanded to 14 days to reach coverage threshold)

incidents.victim: Marquis Software Solutions (bank marketing and compliance vendor) — 670,000+ downstream banking customers across 80+ U.S. banks and credit unions
incidents.vector: Attackers obtained stolen firewall configuration backup files from Stryker's SonicWall cloud portal (a separate earlier breach), used those files to compromise Marquis's network, then deployed ransomware; Marquis alleges SonicWall security failures enabled the initial theft of its firewall credentials.
incidents.impact: More than 670,000 individuals—customers of 80+ community banks and credit unions—had names, dates of birth, postal addresses, Social Security numbers, and bank account and card numbers stolen in an August 2025 breach; the full victim count was disclosed publicly on March 18, 2026. Reports indicate Marquis paid a ransom. Marquis has sued SonicWall over the incident.

incidents.victim: University of Mississippi Medical Center (UMMC) / Passaic County, New Jersey
incidents.attacker: Criminal gang — ransomware (Russia-based operators, per expert assessment)
incidents.vector: Exploitation of a public-facing application to gain initial access, followed by privilege escalation and disabling of security tools, consistent with the ransomware gang's known double-extortion methodology.
incidents.impact: UMMC—Mississippi's only academic medical centre, operating seven hospitals—was forced offline for nine days (February 19–March 2), closing 35 clinics, cancelling elective procedures, and reverting to paper-based operations. The gang demanded an $800,000 ransom and threatened to publish stolen data by March 20. Passaic County, NJ, simultaneously claimed as a victim with phone lines and IT systems taken down; $800,000 ransom also demanded.

incidents.vector: Attackers allegedly compromised administrative credentials and abused Microsoft Intune, a corporate device management console, to remotely wipe tens of thousands of employee devices across the company's global Microsoft environment.
incidents.impact: Global network disruption across offices in 79 countries; attackers claimed to have wiped more than 200,000 servers, mobile devices, and systems and exfiltrated 50 TB of data. Stryker's Lifenet EKG-transmission system—used by paramedics to relay cardiac data to emergency departments—was disrupted, with hospitals in multiple U.S. states temporarily disconnecting from Stryker systems. As of March 17, Stryker was still in the process of restoring systems.

incidents.victim: Unnamed Wisconsin ambulance and emergency medical services provider (largest in the state)
incidents.vector: Method not yet publicly disclosed; breach resulted in unauthorised access to systems holding personal and medical data.
incidents.impact: Approximately 235,000 individuals notified of a data breach affecting personal and health information held by the state's largest ambulance provider.

incidents.victim: Ukrainian government agencies and critical infrastructure (multiple targets)
incidents.attacker: Russia — state-linked espionage group (suspected, per Ukraine CERT-UA; attribution not formally confirmed)
incidents.vector: Phishing emails sent from compromised accounts containing links to public file-sharing services (DropMeFiles, Google Drive); clicking the links executed a PowerShell script deploying previously unknown spying malware dubbed 'Wrecksteel' to extract documents and take screenshots.
incidents.impact: At least three confirmed incidents in March 2026 affecting Ukrainian government agencies and critical infrastructure; the espionage campaign has been active since at least autumn 2024. Stolen data includes text documents, PDFs, images, presentations, and screenshots from infected devices.

incidents.victim: LeakBase cybercrime forum (international law enforcement action)
incidents.attacker: Criminal gang — data extortion (forum operators and users)
incidents.vector: LeakBase operated as an open-web English-language marketplace enabling cybercriminals to buy and sell stolen databases, account credentials, and cybercrime tools sourced from high-profile breaches worldwide.
incidents.impact: U.S. Department of Justice led a 14-country coordinated takedown on March 3–4, 2026, seizing the forum (142,000+ members; 215,000+ messages), its data, and two domains. Arrests, searches, and interviews were conducted in the U.S., Australia, Belgium, Poland, Portugal, Romania, Spain, and the UK. The forum hosted hundreds of millions of stolen credentials and financial account details.

incidents.victim: U.S. federal civilian agencies and critical infrastructure organisations globally (Cisco SD-WAN infrastructure)
incidents.attacker: Unknown (state-suspected espionage group, exploitation active since at least 2023)
incidents.vector: Exploitation of a CVSS 10.0 authentication bypass zero-day (CVE-2026-20127) in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Controller and Manager, allowing unauthenticated remote attackers to obtain administrative privileges; attackers then downgraded device software to exploit a second known vulnerability (CVE-2022-20775) to escalate to root, establish persistence, and laterally move beyond the SD-WAN environment.
incidents.impact: CISA issued Emergency Directive 26-03 requiring all U.S. federal civilian agencies to inventory affected devices, submit forensic logs, apply patches, and assess compromise by March 23, 2026. International partners (Australia, UK, New Zealand) co-sealed threat hunt guidance. Specific victim organisations have not been publicly identified; high-value and critical infrastructure sectors are confirmed targets. Exploitation has been ongoing since at least 2023.

incidents.victim: Multiple high-value critical infrastructure organisations in North America (specific victims undisclosed)
incidents.vector: Exploitation of vulnerable public-facing servers (including a Sitecore ViewState deserialization zero-day, CVE-2025-53690) and use of stolen credentials for initial access, followed by deployment of open-source post-exploitation tools including Earthworm (network tunnelling), SharpHound (Active Directory enumeration), Rubeus (Kerberos abuse), and GoTokenTheft (privilege escalation).
incidents.impact: Multiple intrusions into North American critical infrastructure organisations confirmed since at least 2025; attackers harvested credentials, security configurations, and Active Directory data to create persistent multi-channel access. In at least one case, product-related DLL libraries were exfiltrated, raising the risk of future supply-chain trojanisation. Cisco Talos published findings on January 15, 2026; the campaign is assessed as ongoing.
